serving.natural_language_portion: One standard side salad bowl as served at Sukiya, approximately 120 g of mixed raw vegetables.

nutrient_source.title: Vegetables, mixed, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values adapted from USDA SR Legacy entry for mixed raw vegetables (lettuce, cabbage, cucumber, tomato, carrot blend). No dressing included; values represent plain mixed raw vegetable salad without added dressing. This is the closest defensible approved-source match for a plain Japanese garden salad as served at Sukiya.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a plain Japanese garden salad. GI of 15 is estimated by analogy to raw mixed vegetables and leafy greens as documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283. Raw non-starchy vegetables consistently yield GI values in the range 10–20. GL per serving calculated as (15 × 3.8 × 1.20) / 100 = 0.68, rounded to 0.7.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (~25 mg per 100 g) and high potassium (~210 mg per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Plain Japanese garden salad without added dressing is very low in sodium and provides potassium, making it a suitable vegetable side for individuals managing hypertension; patients should choose low-sodium dressings if any are added.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~15) and low carbohydrate content (~3.8 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Plain Japanese garden salad has a very low GI and minimal carbohydrate load, making it an excellent vegetable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; glycemic load per standard serving is less than 1, posing negligible postprandial glucose impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and high dietary fibre relative to caloric density
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Plain Japanese garden salad is very low in saturated fat and cholesterol-free, and its dietary fibre and antioxidant vitamins (vitamin C, vitamin A) support cardiovascular health; individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid high-fat or high-sodium dressings.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Japanese garden salad (サラダ, sarada) is a simple side dish of mixed raw vegetables commonly served at Japanese casual dining restaurants such as Sukiya in Singapore. It typically consists of shredded cabbage, lettuce leaves, sliced cucumber, cherry tomatoes, and grated or julienned carrot, served chilled without or with a light dressing such as sesame, wafu (Japanese-style), or ginger dressing. The texture is crisp and refreshing, with a mild, clean flavour that complements heavier mains such as gyudon (beef rice bowl) or curry rice. Nutritionally, the plain salad is very low in calories (~20 kcal per 100 g), low in carbohydrates (~3.8 g per 100 g), and provides dietary fibre, potassium, vitamin C, and vitamin A. Its glycemic index is very low (~15), making it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. The dish is naturally vegetarian, vegan, and halal when served without animal-derived dressings. Clinically, it is appropriate for hypertension management due to its low sodium content, and for cardiovascular health due to negligible saturated fat and cholesterol. It is a standard menu item at Sukiya Singapore (Funan outlet) and is priced at approximately SGD 5.00 as a side order.
common_user_queries: What is in a Japanese salad?
Is Japanese salad good for diabetics?

confidence_reason: The food is identified as a plain Japanese garden salad served at Sukiya Singapore. No exact HPB Singapore or USDA entry exists for this specific restaurant preparation. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy mixed raw vegetable data, which is the closest defensible approved-source match. GI is estimated by analogy to raw non-starchy vegetables from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than high because no exact dish-level match was found in a primary regional source.
notes: trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero for plain raw vegetables per USDA SR Legacy; naturally zero for unprocessed plant foods. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-based food with no animal-derived ingredients. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-based food; vitamin B12 is not present in plant foods. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for raw mixed vegetables; naturally zero in non-fortified plant foods. Sentinel value -1 is not used in this record; all micronutrients are reported from USDA SR Legacy data for mixed raw vegetables. Dressing is excluded from nutrient calculations as it varies by restaurant and customer choice. Serving size of 120 g is an estimate based on typical Japanese restaurant side salad portions; exact Sukiya serving weight is not publicly documented. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database data for raw non-starchy vegetables; no direct measurement for this specific preparation exists. Cuisine tags from the input (Beef Dish, Japanese Curry, Grilled Chicken Skewers) reflect the broader Sukiya menu context and are not attributes of this salad item.
